name: Cayce Earth Changes
description: The Cayce Earth Changes are a set of prophecies attributed to Edgar Cayce, a 20th-century American psychic, who allegedly predicted dramatic geological and societal shifts. Cayce's readings, conducted in a trance state, suggested that significant earth changes, including major earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and coastal flooding, would occur in the late 20th and early 21st centuries. Key predictions include the submergence of parts of Japan, the rise of new lands in the Atlantic (potentially related to Atlantis), and significant alterations to the coastlines of North and South America. Proponents often point to increased seismic activity, climate change, and extreme weather events as evidence supporting Cayce's prophecies. Mainstream science largely dismisses these claims, citing the lack of testable hypotheses and verifiable evidence. Geologists and climatologists attribute observed changes to natural processes and anthropogenic climate change, rather than psychic predictions. The vague and interpretable nature of Cayce's prophecies also allows for selective interpretation, making it difficult to falsify them scientifically. Skeptics highlight the absence of specific, accurate dates and locations in many of Cayce's predictions, contributing to their ambiguity and resilience against disproof. Despite the lack of scientific validation, the Cayce Earth Changes remain a popular topic in New Age and alternative spirituality circles.
subtitle: Prophecies of geological and societal upheaval

claims.claim: Parts of Japan will sink into the sea.
claims.evidence: Cayce readings specifically mention the submergence of parts of Japan.
claims.criticism: No widespread geological evidence supports the imminent sinking of major Japanese landmasses. Localized subsidence occurs, but it is unrelated to Cayce's prophecies.

claims.claim: New lands will rise in the Atlantic Ocean, potentially related to the lost continent of Atlantis.
claims.evidence: Cayce readings suggest the reappearance of landmasses in the Atlantic.
claims.criticism: There is no geological evidence to support the rapid emergence of new landmasses in the Atlantic. The concept of Atlantis is considered pseudoscientific by mainstream scholars.

claims.claim: Significant earthquakes and volcanic eruptions will increase globally.
claims.evidence: Cayce readings predict increased seismic and volcanic activity.
claims.criticism: While seismic and volcanic activity fluctuates naturally, there's no statistically significant increase beyond normal geological cycles that can be definitively linked to Cayce's predictions. Increases are explained by better monitoring technology.

keyFigures.name: Edgar Cayce
keyFigures.role: Source of the Earth Change prophecies

timeline.event: Edgar Cayce gives readings containing Earth Change prophecies.
Increased interest and interpretation of Cayce's prophecies in light of environmental concerns.

sources.author: Jeffrey Furst
sources.work: Edgar Cayce's Story of the Origin and Destiny of Man
sources.title: Jeffrey Furst
